nav.sidebarPitchDescription: Move from capture to analysis, refactor, benchmark, and packaging in one place.

home.title: Home
home.description: Build, analyze, benchmark, and package prompts inside a discovery-first workbench designed for iteration.
home.recentDescription: Jump back into the prompts you have touched most recently.
home.inboxDescription: Fast capture items waiting to be promoted into production-ready prompts.
home.collectionsDescription: Group prompts and modules into reusable packs.
home.modulesDescription: Maintain the reusable ingredients behind your best prompts.

prompts.title: Prompts
prompts.libraryDescription: Discover prompt assets quickly, move between status buckets, and keep the strongest entries within easy reach.
prompts.filtersDescription: Use search, filters, and tags together to move through your library with less friction.
prompts.startWriting: Start writing to see a preview.
prompts.emptyFilteredDescription: Try widening your search or clearing the active filters.
prompts.detailDescription: Evolve this prompt through analysis, refactor, versioning, and benchmark comparison.
prompts.detailVersionsDescription: Track immutable snapshots, compare changes, and mark the strongest baseline.
prompts.detailBenchmarkDescription: Inspect the latest scorecard and compare how this prompt evolves against baseline or previous versions.
prompts.detailAgentDescription: Keep analysis and refactor inside the main reading flow so long outputs never stretch a secondary rail.

versions.title: Version History
versions.description: Immutable snapshots created whenever this prompt is saved.
versions.restored: Version restored as the current working copy
versions.noDiff: This version matches the current working copy.

editor.workspaceDescription: Shape prompt content, review live structure, and run the MiniMax agent without leaving the workspace.
editor.metadataDescription: Keep title, intent, model, and tags aligned with the draft you are evolving.
editor.contentDescription: Write, restructure, and prepare the prompt body that powers the workbench.
editor.toolsDescription: Switch between preview, agent insights, version history, and reusable modules without leaving the draft.
editor.contentPlaceholder: Write your prompt here... Use {{variable}} for dynamic values.
editor.sourcePlaceholder: Where did this prompt come from?
editor.tagsPlaceholder: Type and press Enter to add tags
editor.canvasDescription: Draft the core instructions and keep reusable placeholders in double braces.
editor.previewEmptyTitle: Start writing to see a preview.
editor.previewEmptyDescription: We will render the current draft with its variable placeholders so you can inspect tone, structure, and reusable slots.
editor.previewRenderedDescription: Preview the final prompt with fallback values filled in.

inbox.title: Inbox
inbox.pageDescription: Use the inbox as a low-friction capture layer, then promote the best items when they are ready.
inbox.captureDescription: Paste rough ideas quickly so they enter the workflow before you lose them.
inbox.captureContentPlaceholder: Paste or type your prompt here...
inbox.empty: Inbox is empty. Capture a prompt to get started.

modules.title: Modules
modules.pageDescription: Maintain the reusable roles, goals, constraints, and formats that power stronger prompt systems.
modules.inserterEmpty: No modules found. Create modules on the Modules page first.
modules.noModules: No modules found. Create one to get started.

favorites.title: Favorites
favorites.pageDescription: Keep the prompts you reach for most often close at hand so proven building blocks are easy to reuse.
favorites.empty: No favorites yet. Star a prompt to add it here.

archive.title: Archive
archive.pageDescription: Review prompts you have retired from the active workflow, then restore or remove them permanently when needed.

tags.title: Tags
tags.pageDescription: Scan the tag landscape across prompts and modules so you can jump into the right slice of the library faster.
tags.empty: No tags yet. Add tags to your prompts or modules.

playground.title: Agent Playground
playground.description: Paste any prompt text to see how the agent analyzes it. This is a sandbox for testing classification, risk detection, variable extraction, and similarity matching.
playground.placeholder: Paste a prompt here to analyze...
playground.resultsDescription: MiniMax will inspect the prompt as-is and return an instant stateless readout with no saved history.

brief.title: Experiment Brief
brief.description: Use this space to probe how a draft prompt behaves before you save, benchmark, or refactor it elsewhere.
brief.stateless: This run is stateless: it does not write Prompt, AgentHistory, or benchmark data back into the workspace.

workspace.title: Prompt Input
workspace.description: Paste or draft a prompt to inspect.

templates.title: Quick Experiments
templates.description: Load a starter prompt and immediately see how the agent categorizes, scores risk, and extracts variables.

writing.title: Writing assistant
writing.description: A structured prompt for rewriting rough notes into polished prose.
writing.content: You are a senior writing assistant. Rewrite the following rough notes into a polished article intro for {{audience}}. Keep the tone {{tone}}, stay under {{word_limit}} words, and end with one concrete next step.

translation.title: Translation prompt
translation.description: Test bilingual prompts that preserve nuance, terminology, and formatting.
translation.content: Translate the following product announcement from {{source_language}} to {{target_language}}. Preserve bullet structure, keep product names unchanged, and note any phrases that may need localization review.

support.title: Customer support
support.description: Probe how well a support prompt balances empathy, policy, and next actions.
support.content: You are a customer support specialist. Reply to the user issue below using a calm and empathetic tone. Clarify the root cause, suggest the safest next step, and mention any policy limitation that applies.

code.title: Code review prompt
code.description: Inspect prompts that ask for bugs, regressions, and missing tests.
code.content: Review the following code change like a senior engineer. Prioritize bugs, regressions, and missing tests first. Then list medium-risk issues, and end with any open questions that block approval.

outputs.title: What you'll get
outputs.description: Every run produces a structured readout of the prompt's shape, risks, and reusable pieces.
outputs.suggestionsDescription: Recommended category, model, and status to help frame what kind of prompt you're testing.
outputs.variablesDescription: Detected placeholders and input slots that can become explicit variables in a reusable version.
outputs.riskDescription: A risk level plus concrete reasons such as sensitive content, prompt injection patterns, or structural complexity.
outputs.trajectoryDescription: The visible thought / action / observation timeline that shows how MiniMax reached the analysis.

emptyState.title: Ready for the next prompt experiment
emptyState.description: Paste a draft or load a quick experiment on the left to inspect its variables, risk signals, similar prompts, and visible ReAct trajectory.

settings.title: Settings
settings.description: Tune workspace defaults, MiniMax agent behavior, and recovery actions from one control surface.
settings.accessDeniedDescription: This page manages global workspace behavior and is only available to administrators.
settings.accessDeniedBody: Sign in with the administrator password to change defaults, recovery actions, or agent-wide settings.
settings.workspaceDefaultsDescription: Set the baseline view, model, status, and theme that should greet you across the workbench.
settings.themeDescription: Choose how the interface should render when you open the workbench on this machine.
settings.defaultViewDescription: Pick the starting layout for prompt browsing pages.
settings.defaultModelDescription: Set the model label new prompts should inherit by default.
settings.defaultStatusDescription: Choose the lifecycle status assigned to new prompts when they are created.
settings.agentControlsDescription: Control which MiniMax capabilities run automatically and how strict the analysis pipeline should be.
settings.analyzeOnPasteDesc: Trigger analysis immediately after captured text is pasted into the workbench.
settings.providerDescription: Choose which backend label and strategy profile should drive MiniMax-style analysis in this workspace.
settings.analysisDepthDescription: Adjust how deeply the agent should inspect structure, reuse potential, and risk before returning.
settings.riskThresholdDescription: Define the minimum risk severity that should be surfaced as an actionable concern.
settings.confidenceThresholdDescription: Set the minimum confidence score the agent should treat as strong enough for recommendations.
settings.similarityThresholdDescription: Control how similar another prompt must be before it appears as a duplication or inspiration signal.
settings.thresholdHint: Use a value between 0 and 1. Press Enter or blur to save.
settings.dataRecoveryDescription: Export a clean workspace snapshot, restore prompts and modules from backup JSON, or reset settings to defaults.
settings.exportDataDesc: Download prompts, modules, and current settings as a workspace snapshot JSON.
settings.importDataDesc: Restore prompts, modules, and settings from a previously exported workspace snapshot.
settings.resetSettingsDesc: Restore default workspace behavior without deleting prompts, modules, or skills.

footnotes.defaultsDescription: These values seed newly created prompts and browsing surfaces across the workbench.
footnotes.agentDescription: MiniMax analysis, normalization, and module extraction all read from this control surface.
footnotes.thresholdsDescription: Confidence, similarity, and risk thresholds help keep analysis recommendations consistent.
footnotes.dataDescription: Export and import keep a portable snapshot, while reset only affects settings defaults.

search.dialogDescription: Search for a page, prompt, module, collection, or skill.

skills.title: Skills
skills.description: Wrap your strongest prompts into reusable capabilities with a goal, schemas, and an optional collection pack.
skills.empty: No skills yet. Create one from a saved prompt to turn an asset into a reusable capability.
skills.emptyFiltered: No skills match the current health filter yet.

hints.all: Everything in your current capability catalog.
hints.ready: Skills with baseline, benchmark, and recent validation aligned.
hints.watch: Skills that show promise but still need stronger validation.
hints.setup: Skills that still need baseline, benchmark, or live run evidence.

priorities.title: Priority Queue
priorities.description: The next capabilities worth stabilizing based on missing baseline, benchmark, or validation signals.
priorities.empty: Nothing urgent right now. Your active skills already have the core evidence they need.

reasons.needsBaseline: Set a baseline on the entry prompt so future evolution stays anchored.
reasons.needsBenchmark: Run a benchmark to score the current capability before expanding it.
reasons.needsValidation: Capture a live validation run to confirm the rendered prompt still behaves well.
reasons.needsIteration: Recent evaluation still suggests iteration before calling this production-ready.
reasons.stable: This skill already has the key signals needed for reuse.

skills.goalDescription: Define the concrete user outcome this skill should consistently deliver.
skills.schemaDescription: Use simple field contracts to describe what this skill expects and what it should return.
skills.noBenchmark: No benchmark run yet for this skill's entry prompt.
skills.baselineSummaryFallback: This baseline is active and ready to anchor future evolution comparisons.
skills.noBaseline: No baseline version has been chosen for the entry prompt yet.
skills.copyRecentRunPromptFailed: Failed to copy the recent run prompt
skills.noRecentRuns: No recent skill runs recorded yet.

health.title: Capability Health
health.description: Track whether this skill has a baseline, a current benchmark, and a recent validation run.
health.baselineMissing: Choose a baseline version on the entry prompt first.
health.benchmarkRecommended: The latest benchmark recommends production use.
health.benchmarkIterate: The latest benchmark suggests more iteration.
health.benchmarkMissing: Run a benchmark on the entry prompt to score this skill.
health.recentRunMissing: Run the skill once to capture a live validation signal.

headlines.ready: This skill has the core signals needed for confident reuse.
headlines.watch: This skill is promising, but the validation loop is still incomplete.
headlines.setup: This skill still needs baseline, benchmark, or run evidence.

notes.ready: Baseline, benchmark, and recent validation are all present. Continue iterating only when you want to improve quality or scope.
notes.watch: At least one signal is present, but you still need stronger evidence before treating this as stable production capability.
notes.setup: Use the current entry prompt to establish a baseline, run a benchmark, and validate the rendered prompt with a recent skill run.

listSummaries.ready: Baseline, benchmark, and validation are aligned.
listSummaries.watch: Some validation signals exist, but the loop is still incomplete.
listSummaries.setup: This skill still needs benchmark or run evidence.

skills.deleteDescription: This removes the capability shell, but leaves the prompt and collection assets intact.

form.description: Define the capability, point it at an entry prompt, and describe the input/output contract.
form.descriptionPlaceholder: A short explanation of what this skill does.
form.goalPlaceholder: Help a learner improve spoken English in one month with a structured daily plan.
form.notesPlaceholder: Operational notes, guardrails, or rollout reminders.
form.inputSchemaDescription: Describe the inputs this skill expects from a caller or UI.
form.outputSchemaDescription: Describe the outputs this skill should reliably return.
form.schemaEmpty: No schema fields yet. Add the important fields you want to standardize.

run.copyFailed: Failed to copy the rendered prompt
run.inputsDescription: Fill the capability inputs, then generate a concrete prompt instance for execution or review.
run.presetsDescription: Restore your latest values or save named parameter sets for repeated runs.
run.recentRestoredHint: Recent inputs were restored automatically for this skill.
run.noRecentValues: No recent values have been saved for this skill yet.
run.presetNamePlaceholder: Interview prep, weekly review, advanced mode...
run.copyRecentRunPromptFailed: Failed to copy the recent run prompt
run.recentRunLoadedFromLink: Loaded inputs from the selected recent run
run.fieldDescriptionFallback: No description provided for this input yet.
run.noInputs: This skill does not define any input fields yet.
run.analysisDescription: Run a stateless MiniMax analysis against the filled prompt before using it elsewhere.

benchmark.title: Benchmark Scorecard
benchmark.description: Run MiniMax against a saved prompt version to score clarity, reusability, controllability, and deployment readiness.
benchmark.noVersion: Save this prompt first so a version can be benchmarked.
benchmark.compareDescription: Pick two benchmark runs to see whether the latest revision improved.

evolution.title: Latest Evolution Compare
evolution.empty: No evolution comparison is available for this strategy yet.

collections.title: Collections
collections.pageDescription: Package prompts and modules into reusable systems so the best work can be rediscovered and reused quickly.
collections.empty: No collections yet. Create one to group prompts and modules into a reusable pack.
collections.deleteDescription: This removes the collection shell and its item ordering, but does not delete the underlying prompts or modules.
collections.selectItem: Choose a prompt or module first.

form.description: Use collections to bundle prompts and modules into repeatable solution packs.
form.descriptionPlaceholder: What problem does this collection solve?

login.title: Sign in
login.description: Enter your workspace password to unlock Prompt IDE.
login.discoveryDescription: Capture prompts, compare versions, benchmark improvements, and package modules into reusable collections.
login.agentDescription: Analyze, refactor, and benchmark prompts inside one focused workspace built for iteration.

agent.title: Agent Analysis
agent.savePromptFirst: Save the prompt before running agent analysis.

refactor.title: Agent Refactor
refactor.description: Use MiniMax to prepare a cleaner prompt draft, reusable variables, and module extraction suggestions.
refactor.saveFirst: Save the prompt before running or applying refactor suggestions.
refactor.draftApplied: Refactor draft applied as the new working copy

diff.title: Draft Diff
diff.description: Review the current working copy against the MiniMax refactor draft before applying it.
diff.noChanges: The refactor draft already matches the current working copy.

evolution.title: Evolution Benchmark
evolution.description: After accepting a refactor, compare the new version against your baseline or previous version.
evolution.empty: Apply the draft or variables to generate an automatic benchmark comparison.

draft.title: Cleaned Draft
draft.description: A read-only draft prepared by MiniMax. Applying it updates the prompt and creates a new version snapshot.

variables.title: Suggested Variables
variables.description: Reusable variable metadata extracted from the refactor proposal.

modules.title: Extracted Modules
modules.description: Select the reusable building blocks you want to save into the module library.
modules.none: No reusable module suggestions were generated.
modules.selectHint: Select at least one module suggestion to create it.

quickAdd.title: Add Created Modules To A Collection
quickAdd.description: Drop the newly created modules into an existing collection so they immediately become part of a reusable pack.
quickAdd.createdAndAdded: Created a collection and added {count} module(s)
quickAdd.noCollections: No collections yet. Create one first, then come back and add these modules.

risk.prompt-type: Prompt targets a specialized task type
risk.complexity-high: Prompt complexity is high and may need tighter control
risk.meta-prompt: Prompt behaves like a meta-prompt or prompt generator
risk.requires-file-system-output: Task expects file system output or generated artifacts
risk.requires-multiple-templates: Task likely needs multiple coordinated templates
risk.requires-directory-structure: Task likely needs a directory or project structure
risk.lifecycle-management-required: Task needs lifecycle management across multiple steps
risk.testing-framework-required: Task likely requires a testing or verification framework
risk.external-inspiration-not-direct-copy: Use external inspiration carefully without direct copying
risk.actionableGoal: Prompt asks for an actionable goal

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.description: The page you are looking for does not exist or may have been moved.

groups.gettingStarted: Start quickly and understand the product surface before exploring deeper workflows.
groups.coreFeatures: Learn the prompt workspace, reusable building blocks, and organization patterns.
groups.aiTools: Use MiniMax-powered analysis, refactor, and benchmark tools with confidence.
groups.advanced: Move beyond the basics into skills, versions, inbox workflows, and archival patterns.
groups.reference: Find practical reference material for settings, shortcuts, and common questions.

welcome.title: Welcome to Prompt IDE Documentation
welcome.description: Learn how to capture, refine, analyze, benchmark, and package prompts inside Prompt IDE.

callouts.tip: Use the docs sidebar to jump between workflows while keeping the current article in view.
callouts.note: This first batch focuses on navigation, layout, and documentation primitives rather than full article content.
callouts.warning: All documentation routes stay behind the existing authentication flow, just like the rest of Prompt IDE.

placeholder.description: {section} is scaffolded and ready for the next content batch.
placeholder.overview: This page will introduce the core workflow, UI surface, and expected outcomes for {section}.
placeholder.comingNext: The next documentation batch will replace this placeholder with complete guidance, examples, and screenshots for {section}.

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.intro: Prompt IDE is a private prompt R&D workspace for organizing, iterating on, and improving AI prompts over time.

whatIs.title: What is Prompt IDE?
whatIs.content: Prompt IDE brings prompt capture, editing, analysis, benchmarking, and packaging into one authenticated workspace. It is built for people who want more structure than a notes app and more flexibility than a fixed prompt gallery.

capabilities.title: Core capabilities
capabilities.content: The product is organized around the full prompt lifecycle, from first draft to reusable asset.

promptManagement.title: Prompt management.
promptManagement.content: Store prompts as first-class assets with status, model, category, tags, notes, and source metadata. Browse them in multiple views, search quickly, and move them between inbox, production, and archive.

editor.title: Editor.
editor.content: Use a purpose-built editor with metadata, variable-aware content editing, preview, modules, versions, and AI tools in one place. The editor is optimized for iterative prompt work rather than generic text editing.

analysisRefactor.title: AI analysis and refactor.
analysisRefactor.content: Run guided analysis and refactor flows powered by MiniMax-backed agents. The system can review prompt quality, surface risks, suggest metadata, and propose cleaner drafts or reusable modules.

versioningBenchmark.title: Versioning and benchmarks.
versioningBenchmark.content: Every meaningful save can create a version snapshot. Benchmarks score prompt quality across several dimensions and compare the current version against a baseline or previous version.

modules.title: Module reuse.
modules.content: Break repeated prompt patterns into reusable modules such as role, goal, or output format snippets. Insert them from the editor instead of rewriting the same instructions again and again.

skills.title: Skills.
skills.content: Wrap prompts into reusable runnable tools with schemas, presets, and execution flows. Skills help teams reuse complex prompt templates without exposing all the raw prompt authoring steps every time.

prompts.title: Prompt management.
prompts.content: Store prompts as first-class assets with status, model, category, tags, notes, and source metadata. Browse them in multiple views, search quickly, and move them between inbox, production, and archive.

analysis.title: AI analysis and refactor.
analysis.content: Run guided analysis and refactor flows powered by MiniMax-backed agents. The system can review prompt quality, surface risks, suggest metadata, and propose cleaner drafts or reusable modules.

benchmark.title: Versioning and benchmarks.
benchmark.content: Every meaningful save can create a version snapshot. Benchmarks score prompt quality across several dimensions and compare the current version against a baseline or previous version.

audience.title: Who is this for?
audience.content: Prompt IDE is designed for solo AI builders, prompt engineers, and teams who want a repeatable way to manage prompt assets. It is especially helpful when prompts need review, version history, benchmarking, or packaging into reusable building blocks.

foundation.title: Tech foundation
foundation.content: The app is built on a modern Next.js stack, supports Chinese and English interfaces, works in light and dark themes, and has responsive layouts for desktop and mobile use.

introduction.calloutTip: If this is your first time in the app, start with the Quick Tour page before diving into individual tools.

loginRoles.title: Login & Roles
loginRoles.intro: Prompt IDE uses a simple password-based sign-in flow. After you enter a password, the server determines which role that password belongs to and creates the session for you.

loggingIn.title: Logging in
loggingIn.content: There is no username field. Enter a valid password on the login page and select Unlock. The app checks the password server-side and signs you in with the matching role.

roles.title: Roles
roles.content: Prompt IDE currently supports two roles with different levels of control.
roles.adminContent: Admins can delete prompts, modules, collections, and skills. They can also access Settings, including export, import, and reset actions.
roles.memberContent: Members can browse and edit day-to-day content, but they cannot delete assets or open the administrative settings area.

errors.title: Error messages
errors.content: The login form surfaces clear messages for the most common sign-in problems.
errors.invalidPassword: An incorrect password shows a password mismatch message.
errors.emptyPassword: Submitting an empty or whitespace-only password shows a dedicated empty-password message.
errors.configError: If the server is missing password configuration, the page shows a configuration error instead of letting the request silently fail.

session.title: Session
session.content: After login, the session cookie stays valid for 30 days unless you sign out earlier. Session handling is automatic, and you can sign out from the account area near the bottom of the sidebar.

security.title: Security
security.content: Authentication uses an httpOnly session cookie so client-side scripts cannot read it directly. In production the cookie is marked secure for HTTPS, and password checks use timing-safe comparison helpers on the server.

loginRoles.calloutNote: If you see a configuration error on the login screen, the server passwords are not configured correctly. Contact the workspace administrator rather than trying random passwords.

quickTour.title: Quick Tour
quickTour.intro: The interface is organized so you can move from capture to editing, analysis, reuse, and packaging without losing context.

layout.title: App layout
layout.content: Most screens follow the same pattern: a navigation sidebar on the left, a top bar for search and preferences, and a main content area for the page you are working on.

sidebar.title: Sidebar navigation
sidebar.content: The sidebar groups the main product areas so you can jump between capture, authoring, reuse, and administration.
sidebar.utilityTitle: The utility area gives you quick access to tools and workspace-level pages.

home.title: Home.
home.content: Start from the dashboard with stats, recent prompts, tags, and shortcuts into the rest of the workspace.

prompts.title: Prompts.
prompts.content: Browse the main prompt library with filters, search, and sorting.

editor.title: Editor.
editor.content: Create a new prompt or continue editing an existing one in the dedicated authoring workspace.

inbox.title: Inbox.
inbox.content: Review new ideas, quick captures, and cloned prompts before promoting them into production.

modules.title: Modules.
modules.content: Manage reusable prompt fragments such as roles, goals, and output formats.

collections.title: Collections.
collections.content: Group prompts and modules into themed packs for reuse and sharing.

skills.title: Skills.
skills.content: Package prompts into runnable tools with schemas, presets, and execution history.

favorites.title: Favorites.
favorites.content: Return to the prompts you care about most without changing their underlying status.

archive.title: Archive.
archive.content: Review archived prompts and restore or permanently remove them when appropriate.

tags.title: Tags.
tags.content: See which tags are used across the library and jump into filtered prompt views.

playground.title: Playground.
playground.content: Experiment with prompt analysis before creating a saved prompt record.

docs.title: Docs.
docs.content: Open the product documentation area you are reading now.

settings.title: Settings.
settings.content: Adjust workspace defaults and data management controls if you are an admin.

topBar.title: Top bar features
topBar.content: The top bar focuses on fast navigation and personal preferences.
topBar.search: Press Ctrl+K or Cmd+K to open the command-style search dialog for pages, prompts, modules, collections, and skills.
topBar.theme: Use the theme toggle to switch between light and dark modes.
topBar.locale: Use the language switcher to swap between English and Chinese without leaving your current context.

dashboard.title: Home dashboard
dashboard.content: The Home page highlights key counts such as total prompts, inbox items, favorites, and modules. It also shows recent prompts and quick entry points so you can continue work from where you left off.

quickTour.calloutTip: Try Ctrl+K or Cmd+K first. It is the fastest way to understand how pages and assets are connected across the workspace.

prompts.title: Prompts
prompts.intro: Prompts are the core assets in Prompt IDE. Each record combines the prompt text itself with the metadata needed to review, reuse, and improve it over time.

whatIs.title: What is a prompt asset?
whatIs.content: A prompt asset is more than raw text. It can include a title, description, full content, variables, tags, status, category, preferred model, notes, and source information so the prompt stays understandable as it evolves.

statuses.title: Prompt statuses
statuses.content: Statuses help you separate draft work from production-ready prompts and retired material.

inbox.title: Inbox.
inbox.content: New or still-reviewing prompts usually begin here so you can refine them before wider use.

production.title: Production.
production.content: Prompts in active use or considered ready for regular work live in this state.

archived.title: Archived.
archived.content: Archived prompts are kept for history and recovery but removed from day-to-day active lists.

browsing.title: Browsing prompts
browsing.content: The library supports multiple ways to scan and narrow down prompt records.
browsing.views: Switch between a card view and a denser list view on larger screens.
browsing.filters: Filter the library by status, preferred model, and tags.
browsing.sorting: Sort by recently updated, recently created, alphabetical order, or recent use depending on the screen.
browsing.search: Search matches titles and descriptions in real time so you can jump to the right prompt quickly.

detail.title: Prompt detail page
detail.content: Each prompt has a detail page that combines the full text with reference data and quick actions.
detail.fullContent: Read the full prompt body and copy it with one click.
detail.variables: Review detected variables and their saved metadata.
detail.notes: Keep implementation notes or reminders alongside the prompt.
detail.metadata: See status, model, category, source, and timestamps in the metadata sidebar.
detail.tags: Open related tags directly from the tag chips shown on the page.

actions.title: Common actions
actions.content: Prompt detail and list views expose the most common workflow actions.

items.copy: Copy the prompt content to the clipboard.
items.favorite: Add or remove the prompt from Favorites without changing its workflow state.
items.edit: Open the editor to refine the prompt.
items.promote: Move the prompt from Inbox into Production when it is ready.
items.clone: Create a new Inbox copy to explore a variation safely.
items.createSkill: Create a skill using the prompt as the entry prompt.
items.archiveRestore: Archive a prompt when it is no longer active, or restore it later.
items.delete: Delete the prompt permanently if your role allows destructive actions.

prompts.calloutNote: Deleting a prompt cannot be undone and requires admin permissions. Use Archive first if you are not completely sure.

editor.title: Editor
editor.intro: The editor is the main authoring workspace for prompt creation and iteration. It keeps metadata, content, versioning, AI tools, and module insertion in one screen.

layout.content: The editor uses a two-column layout. The left side focuses on metadata and the main content body, while the right side hosts Preview, Agent, Versions, and Modules tabs for context-aware tooling.

metadata.content: The form separates required fields from optional enrichment fields so you can save quickly and improve structure later.
metadata.required: Title and content are required before a prompt can be saved.
metadata.optional: Description, preferred model, status, category, source, tags, notes, and other metadata remain optional but useful.

content.content: Write the main prompt body in the content editor and use {{variableName}} placeholders where the prompt should stay reusable. Use Ctrl+S or Cmd+S to save without leaving the keyboard.

tools.content: The right-hand tool panel keeps the supporting workflows close to the prompt you are editing.
tools.preview: Preview shows a rendered version of the prompt, using default variable values when available and placeholder brackets when not.
tools.agent: Agent contains the analysis and refactor flows for saved prompts.
tools.versions: Versions shows the prompt history and restoration tools.
tools.modules: Modules lets you browse reusable snippets and insert them into the current prompt.

saving.content: Saving does more than persist text. It also updates the prompt lifecycle records behind the scenes.
saving.create: Saving a new prompt creates the prompt record and its initial version snapshot.
saving.update: Saving an existing prompt updates the current fields and records a new version snapshot.
saving.dirty: Any unsaved edit marks the prompt as having unsaved changes. While that state is active, analysis and refactor actions stay disabled until you save again.

editor.calloutWarning: AI analysis and refactor only run on saved prompts. If the buttons are disabled or the page asks you to save first, save the prompt and try again.
editor.calloutTip: When you insert a module, the editor tries to place it at the current cursor position. If the editor is not focused, the module is appended to the end.

variables.title: Variables
variables.intro: Variables let one prompt template adapt to many situations. Instead of duplicating prompts for every context, you can parameterize the parts that change.

whatIs.title: What are variables?
whatIs.content: Variables are named placeholders embedded directly inside prompt content. They make prompts reusable across roles, topics, products, formats, and other changing inputs.

syntax.title: Variable syntax
syntax.content: Prompt IDE detects variables written in the {{variableName}} form. Variable names should use letters, numbers, and underscores so they stay predictable across the workspace.

detection.title: Automatic detection
detection.content: You do not have to register variables by hand before using them. The editor scans the prompt body and extracts variable entries automatically.
detection.name: Name is derived from the placeholder itself and is not manually renamed in the variable list.
detection.description: Description is optional and helps explain what the variable should contain.
detection.defaultValue: Default value is optional and is used for rendering previews.

preview.title: Preview rendering
preview.content: Preview uses saved variable metadata to render a readable version of the prompt before you run it elsewhere.
preview.withDefault: If a variable has a default value, Preview substitutes that value into the rendered prompt.
preview.withoutDefault: If no default is set, Preview renders the placeholder as [variableName] so missing inputs stay visible.

example.title: Example
example.content: A simple template can expose several change points while keeping the overall instruction stable.
example.code: You are a {{role}}. Please analyze the following {{topic}} and provide {{outputFormat}}.

variables.calloutTip: Detected variables also feed into AI analysis and the skill runner, so clear variable names make later workflows easier to understand.

modules.title: Modules
modules.intro: Modules are reusable prompt fragments. They help you standardize the repeated parts of your prompting style without forcing you to duplicate the same blocks in every prompt.

whatIs.title: What are modules?
whatIs.content: A module is a saved snippet such as a role definition, a goal statement, or an output format block. Modules can be managed independently and then inserted into prompts from the editor.

types.title: Module types
types.content: The module library organizes snippets by purpose so you can find the right kind of building block quickly.

role.title: Role.
role.content: Define who the model should act as.

goal.title: Goal.
goal.content: Clarify the outcome or task the prompt should accomplish.

constraint.title: Constraint.
constraint.content: Set boundaries, exclusions, or operating rules.

outputFormat.title: Output Format.
outputFormat.content: Describe how the answer should be structured.

style.title: Style.
style.content: Set tone, voice, or stylistic expectations.

selfCheck.title: Self Check.
selfCheck.content: Add a final review or validation instruction for the model.

browsing.title: Browsing modules
browsing.content: Browse modules by type and scan cards for title, type, updated time, content preview, and tags before inserting or editing them.

editing.title: Creating and editing modules
editing.content: Use the module dialog to create or revise reusable fragments.
editing.required: Title, type, and content are required.
editing.tags: Tags are optional and can be entered inline with Enter or commas.
editing.aiGenerated: Some modules may originate from AI refactor suggestions and can still be edited manually afterward.

inserting.title: Inserting modules in the editor
inserting.content: Open the Modules tab in the editor, filter by type if needed, and select Insert on the module you want. The content is inserted into the prompt with spacing handled for you.

modules.calloutNote: Deleting a module requires admin permissions. Removing a module from the library does not retroactively change prompts where that module was already inserted as text.

collections.title: Collections
collections.intro: Collections group related prompts and modules into reusable packs. They are useful when a workflow depends on several assets working together.

whatIs.title: What are collections?
whatIs.content: A collection is a curated set of prompt and module records assembled around a topic, workflow, or learning goal. It helps you package related assets without copying them into one large prompt.

types.title: Collection types
types.content: Collections can be categorized to match how the pack will be used.

workflow.title: Workflow.
workflow.content: For step-by-step operational flows or repeatable prompt pipelines.

toolkit.title: Toolkit.
toolkit.content: For sets of reusable assets that support a broader job or domain.

learning.title: Learning.
learning.content: For examples, references, or teaching-oriented bundles.

create.title: Creating a collection
create.content: Start a collection from the collection list, then provide a required title, an optional description, and a type that matches the pack's purpose.

adding.title: Adding items
adding.content: Use the detail view to add assets into the collection.
adding.prompts: You can add prompt records from the prompt library.
adding.modules: You can also add reusable modules from the module library.
adding.noDuplicates: The same item cannot be added twice to the same collection.

detail.title: Collection detail
detail.content: The detail page lists each item with a type marker and link so you can jump into the underlying prompt or module. Admin users can remove individual items from the collection when needed.

collections.calloutTip: If AI refactor creates reusable modules, you can add them to an existing collection right away to keep related assets together.

tags.title: Tags
tags.intro: Tags are a lightweight way to organize content across prompts and modules. They work across the library instead of being limited to a single page or view.

whatIs.title: What are tags?
whatIs.content: Tags are shared labels used to group related prompts and modules. They make filtering and discovery faster when your library grows beyond a handful of assets.

create.title: Creating tags
create.content: Tags are created inline while editing prompts or modules rather than through a separate admin screen.
create.inline: Type a tag name directly in the tag input while editing a prompt or module.
create.confirm: Press Enter or type a comma to confirm the tag.
create.lowercase: Tags are normalized to lowercase automatically.

overview.title: Tags overview page
overview.content: The Tags page aggregates all known tags and shows how often each one is used, including totals split across prompts and modules.

filtering.title: Tag filtering
filtering.content: Use tag chips and tag pages to jump into prompt views filtered by a specific label. The Home page also highlights frequently used tags as quick entry points.

tags.calloutNote: There is currently no dedicated tag rename or bulk-delete workflow. To remove a tag completely, you need to remove it from every prompt and module that uses it.

playground.title: Playground
playground.intro: The Playground is the fastest place to test an idea before you commit it to the prompt library. It focuses on quick analysis instead of long-term asset management.

whatIs.title: What is the Playground?
whatIs.content: Playground is an unsaved analysis workspace where you can paste prompt text, try starter templates, and inspect AI feedback without creating a prompt record first.

howToUse.title: How to use it
howToUse.content: A typical Playground run is short and experimental.
howToUse.step1: Type or paste a prompt into the input area.
howToUse.step2: Optionally start from a built-in template such as writing, translation, support, or code.
howToUse.step3: Select Analyze to run the AI review.
howToUse.step4: Read the resulting suggestions, risks, variables, and similar-prompt signals before deciding whether to save the idea elsewhere.

comparison.title: Playground vs. Editor
comparison.content: Both areas help you improve prompts, but they serve different points in the workflow.
comparison.playground: Playground is temporary and analysis-first. It does not create saved prompt records or version history.
comparison.editor: Editor is for durable prompt work. It saves metadata, versions, modules, and AI-assisted changes into the main workspace.

output.title: Analysis output
output.content: The Playground analysis mirrors the main analysis flow closely enough for early exploration.
output.category: It can suggest categories, models, or workflow metadata based on the text.
output.variables: It identifies likely variables and parameterization opportunities.
output.risk: It highlights risk signals or ambiguity that may reduce prompt reliability.
output.similar: It can surface similar prompt candidates from the saved library.

playground.calloutTip: Use Playground when you want a fast reality check before turning an idea into a saved prompt with versions and metadata.

analysis.title: Agent Analysis
analysis.intro: Agent Analysis is the deeper AI review workflow for saved prompts. It combines metadata suggestions, risk review, variable extraction, and similarity checks into one report.

whatIs.title: What is AI analysis?
whatIs.content: This feature runs a MiniMax-backed analysis flow over a saved prompt. The goal is to help you spot quality gaps, risky assumptions, unclear structure, and metadata suggestions before the prompt goes further.

howToRun.title: How to run it
howToRun.content: Open a saved prompt in the editor, switch to the Agent tab, then choose Analysis. The prompt must already be saved and free of unsaved changes before analysis can start.

results.title: Analysis results
results.content: The result panel combines several perspectives into one review.
results.confidence: Confidence indicates how certain the analysis is about its classification and recommendations.
results.risk: Risk level summarizes major concerns and explains what may reduce prompt reliability or safety.
results.category: Metadata suggestions can include category, model, status, and tags.
results.variables: The analysis can highlight variables that should be formalized for reuse.
results.similar: Similar prompt detection helps you see whether the workspace already contains a close match.
results.modules: Module candidates point out repeated fragments that could become reusable modules.

trajectory.title: Agent trajectory
trajectory.content: You can inspect the reasoning trace behind the result. Steps are grouped into thought, action, and observation so the AI's path is more transparent than a single opaque answer.

analysis.calloutNote: Analysis uses a MiniMax-backed model flow and may take several seconds to complete. If the AI service is temporarily unavailable, the app shows a friendly error instead of exposing low-level network details.

refactor.title: Agent Refactor
refactor.intro: Agent Refactor proposes an improved version of the current prompt instead of only reviewing it. It helps you move from diagnosis to a concrete revision plan.

whatIs.title: What is AI refactor?
whatIs.content: Refactor runs an AI-assisted improvement pass over the current prompt and returns a structured proposal for clearer content, better metadata, reusable variables, and possible modules.

howToRun.title: How to run it
howToRun.content: Open a saved prompt in the editor, switch to Agent, then choose Refactor. As with analysis, the prompt must be saved and have no unsaved edits before the run begins.

proposal.title: What the proposal includes
proposal.content: A refactor result is more than a rewritten paragraph. It bundles several kinds of suggested changes together.
proposal.summary: A summary explains what changed and why the AI believes the prompt can be improved.
proposal.draft: A cleaned-up draft proposes improved title, description, content, and tags.
proposal.variables: Variable suggestions extract reusable parameters from the current prompt.
proposal.modules: Module suggestions identify fragments worth saving as standalone modules.

diff.title: Diff view
diff.content: The comparison panel shows only the fields that changed, making it easier to evaluate the proposal before you apply anything.

apply.title: Applying changes
apply.content: You do not have to accept everything at once. The editor exposes separate application actions for the main parts of the proposal.
apply.draft: Apply draft accepts the proposed title, description, content, and tags and saves them as a new version.
apply.variables: Apply variables accepts the suggested variable definitions and also creates a new version snapshot.
apply.modules: Create modules turns selected module suggestions into independent module records.
apply.collections: After modules are created, you can add them straight into an existing collection.

refactor.calloutWarning: Applying a draft or variable set creates a new version snapshot. Review the diff carefully before you accept the change.
refactor.calloutTip: After a refactor, run or review benchmark comparisons to see whether the revised version is actually moving in the right direction.

benchmark.title: Benchmark
benchmark.intro: Benchmarks give you a more standardized way to evaluate prompt quality across versions. Instead of relying only on intuition, you can compare the same prompt against earlier states using consistent dimensions.

whatIs.title: What are benchmarks?
whatIs.content: A benchmark is an AI-generated evaluation report tied to a specific prompt version. It summarizes overall quality and scores how ready that version is for dependable use.

scores.title: Five score dimensions
scores.content: Each benchmark includes an overall score and four supporting dimensions to explain where the result comes from.

overall.title: Overall.
overall.content: A combined signal that summarizes the prompt's general quality.

clarity.title: Clarity.
clarity.content: How clearly the prompt communicates intent and expected behavior.

reusability.title: Reusability.
reusability.content: How well the prompt can be reused across similar tasks without major rewriting.

controllability.title: Controllability.
controllability.content: How effectively the prompt constrains outputs and exposes useful parameters.

deploymentReadiness.title: Deployment readiness.
deploymentReadiness.content: How close the prompt is to being safe and structured enough for regular production use.

run.title: Running a benchmark
run.content: You can trigger a benchmark from prompt detail views or other benchmark panels. The result is stored against a specific version snapshot so later comparisons stay meaningful.

evolution.title: Evolution comparison
evolution.content: Benchmark comparison shows how the current version changes relative to a baseline or previous version. Score deltas make improvements and regressions easy to spot, and the report can suggest whether the prompt is ready or should keep iterating.

strategies.title: Comparison strategies
strategies.content: Comparison mode controls which earlier version is used as the reference point.

items.baseline: Baseline compares against the version explicitly marked as the prompt's baseline.
items.previousVersion: Previous Version compares against the immediately earlier snapshot.
items.auto: Auto prefers the baseline when one exists and falls back to the previous version otherwise.

benchmark.calloutTip: If you care about long-running quality tracking, mark a strong version as the baseline first so later benchmark results have a stable reference point.

skills.title: Skills
skills.intro: Skills turn prompts into reusable runnable tools. They are useful when a prompt needs structured input, repeatable execution, and shareable presets rather than ad hoc copy-and-paste use.

whatIs.title: What is a skill?
whatIs.content: A skill wraps a prompt in a runnable layer that includes metadata, schemas, status, and execution support. It is the bridge between prompt authoring and practical day-to-day use.

structure.title: Skill structure
structure.content: Each skill record combines descriptive metadata with the prompt and schema information needed to run it consistently.
structure.name: Name identifies the skill in lists and search results.
structure.description: Description explains what the skill does and when to use it.
structure.goal: Goal captures the intended outcome of the skill.
structure.entryPrompt: The entry prompt points to the prompt that powers the skill.
structure.model: A preferred model can be stored with the skill configuration.
structure.schema: Input and output schemas describe the expected data shape in JSON form.
structure.status: Status tracks whether the skill is draft, active, or archived.

create.title: Creating a skill
create.content: You can create a skill from the Skills area or start from a prompt detail page when you already know which prompt should serve as the entry point.

runner.title: Skill runner
runner.content: The runner is where a skill becomes executable.
runner.fields: The runner derives usable input fields from the prompt variables and skill schema.
runner.preview: You can preview the rendered prompt before execution.
runner.run: After filling the inputs, start a run directly from the runner.
runner.results: Recent output shows the analysis-style result and related execution information.

presets.title: Presets
presets.content: Presets let you save commonly used input combinations and reload them later. You can also turn recent runs into reusable presets to speed up repeated tasks.

skills.calloutTip: Skills are a strong fit when you want teammates to use a prompt workflow as a tool without editing the raw prompt every time.

versions.title: Versions
versions.intro: Version history records how a prompt evolves over time. Instead of overwriting yesterday's work, Prompt IDE keeps immutable snapshots that make comparison and recovery safer.

whatIs.title: What is version history?
whatIs.content: Every saved revision can create a new prompt version. This gives you a stable record of how the prompt changed, what the prompt looked like at each step, and which version a benchmark belongs to.

snapshots.title: What snapshots store
snapshots.content: A version snapshot preserves the full prompt state, including title, description, content, status, source, model, category, tags, notes, and variables rather than only the text body.

viewing.title: Viewing versions
viewing.content: Versions are shown in descending order so the latest snapshot appears first. The editor and prompt detail views expose version history along with timestamps, version numbers, and summary information.

baseline.title: Baseline
baseline.content: One version can be marked as the baseline for a prompt. Benchmark comparison uses that baseline as the preferred reference point whenever available.

restore.title: Restoring a version
restore.content: When you restore a previous version, the app shows the differences between the current prompt and the selected snapshot. Confirming the restore applies the older content as the new current state and records that restore itself as a fresh version.

versions.calloutNote: Restoring a version does not erase the versions that came after it. History remains intact, and the restore becomes another step in that history.

inbox.title: Inbox
inbox.intro: Inbox is the capture-first area for prompts that are not yet production-ready. It helps you store ideas quickly and clean them up later.

whatIs.title: What is the Inbox?
whatIs.content: Inbox is the holding area for new prompt ideas, rough drafts, and cloned experiments. It is meant for collection and triage rather than final publication.

enter.title: How items enter the Inbox
enter.content: Several workflows intentionally send prompts into Inbox first.
enter.newPrompt: Brand-new prompts default to the inbox state.
enter.quickCapture: The Inbox page includes a quick-capture area for saving raw ideas fast.
enter.clone: Cloned prompts also return to Inbox so the copy can evolve independently.

capture.title: Quick capture
capture.content: Use the quick-capture controls at the top of the Inbox page when you want to record an idea before you have the time to structure its metadata properly.

manage.title: Managing inbox items
manage.content: Inbox items can be promoted or redirected once you know what should happen next.
manage.promote: Promote moves an item from inbox to production when it is ready for active use.
manage.edit: Edit opens the item in the editor so you can improve structure and content.
manage.archive: Archive removes an item from active flows without deleting the record immediately.
manage.copy: Copy lets you grab the text quickly for use elsewhere.

inbox.calloutTip: Capture first, polish later. Inbox works best when you treat it as a fast landing zone instead of waiting for every new idea to be perfectly structured.

archiveFavorites.title: Archive & Favorites
archiveFavorites.intro: Archive and Favorites solve two different needs: removing prompts from daily views without deleting them, and keeping a personal shortlist of prompts you want to revisit often.

archive.title: Archive
archive.content: Archive is the soft-delete path for prompts that should leave active lists but remain available for history and recovery.
archive.softDelete: Archived prompts no longer appear in main active browsing flows such as the prompt library, inbox, or favorites.
archive.restore: Restoring an archived prompt brings it back into active use, typically with production status.
archive.deleteForever: Permanent deletion is reserved for admins and cannot be undone.

favorites.title: Favorites
favorites.content: Favorite status is independent from prompt status, so you can favorite a production prompt without changing its workflow state.
favorites.orthogonal: Favorites are orthogonal to inbox, production, and archive states.
favorites.add: Add a favorite from prompt cards or detail pages using the star control.
favorites.remove: Select the star again to remove a prompt from Favorites.
favorites.visible: The Favorites page only shows favorited prompts that are not archived.

archiveFavorites.calloutNote: If a prompt is archived, it disappears from Favorites even if it was starred earlier. Archive always wins over the favorite shortcut views.

settings.title: Settings
settings.intro: Settings gives administrators control over workspace defaults, AI behavior, and data management tasks. Member accounts do not see this area.

access.title: Accessing Settings
access.content: Settings is available from the utility area of the sidebar and is only visible to admins.

workspace.title: Workspace defaults
workspace.content: Workspace defaults influence how new records and daily views behave before you override them manually.
workspace.theme: Theme can follow the system setting or be forced into light or dark mode.
workspace.defaultView: Default view controls whether prompt browsing starts in card or list layout.
workspace.defaultModel: Default model sets the preferred model value used for new prompts.
workspace.defaultStatus: Default status decides whether new prompts begin in inbox, production, or archived state.

agent.title: Agent controls
agent.content: The agent section tunes AI-assisted behavior and the thresholds behind analysis workflows.
agent.enabled: You can enable or disable the AI agent features globally.
agent.autoAnalyze: Automatic analysis can be turned on for relevant workflows.
agent.analyzeOnPaste: Paste-time analysis can be enabled or disabled.
agent.normalization: Text normalization can be toggled as part of the AI preprocessing flow.
agent.moduleExtraction: Module extraction can be enabled so the system suggests reusable modules.
agent.analysisDepth: Analysis depth can be set to quick, standard, or deep.
agent.thresholds: Risk, confidence, and similarity thresholds let you tune how strict the AI workflows should be.

data.title: Data management
data.content: Administrative data actions are grouped together so you can protect and move workspace data deliberately.
data.export: Export downloads workspace data, including prompts, modules, and settings, as JSON.
data.import: Import restores workspace data from a compatible JSON file.
data.reset: Reset restores settings to their default values.

settings.calloutWarning: Reset actions cannot be undone. Export a backup first if there is any chance you will want to return to the previous state.

keyboardShortcuts.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
keyboardShortcuts.intro: Prompt IDE keeps keyboard shortcuts intentionally small for now, focusing on the actions people use most often.

global.title: Global shortcuts
global.search: Ctrl+K or Cmd+K opens the global search and command dialog.

editor.title: Editor shortcuts
editor.save: Ctrl+S or Cmd+S saves the current prompt in the editor.

general.title: General interactions
general.content: Standard browser and dialog controls still apply. Tab moves focus, Enter confirms focused actions, and Escape closes dialogs or sheets where supported.

keyboardShortcuts.calloutNote: The shortcut set will likely grow over time, but the current experience intentionally stays lightweight and easy to remember.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.intro: These are the questions new users tend to hit first when they start working in Prompt IDE.

grayButton.title: Why is the AI Analysis button gray?
grayButton.answer1: The most common reason is that the current prompt still has unsaved changes. Analysis and refactor flows only run against a saved prompt version, so the editor disables those actions until the prompt is saved.
grayButton.answer2: Save the prompt first, wait for the unsaved badge to disappear, and then try the analysis action again.

serviceUnavailable.title: What should I do if the report says 'AI service temporarily unavailable'?
serviceUnavailable.answer1: That message usually means the app could not reach the external AI service at that moment. It does not mean your prompt has been deleted or corrupted.
serviceUnavailable.answer2: Wait a little and retry the action. Other parts of the workspace should continue working normally while the external service is unavailable.

promoteInbox.title: How do I promote a prompt from Inbox to production?
promoteInbox.answer1: Open the prompt from Inbox or from its detail page and use the Promote action. This changes the prompt state from inbox to production.
promoteInbox.answer2: It is a good idea to review the prompt, save the latest edits, and confirm the metadata before you promote it.

deletePermission.title: Why can't I see delete buttons?
deletePermission.answer1: Destructive actions are restricted to admin users. If you are signed in as a member, the interface intentionally hides delete controls for prompts, modules, collections, and skills.
deletePermission.answer2: If you expected to have admin access, ask the workspace administrator to confirm which password and role you should be using.

backupData.title: How do I back up my data?
backupData.answer1: Open Settings and use the export option in the data management area. The app downloads a JSON snapshot of the workspace data that can be stored elsewhere.
backupData.answer2: Back up before large cleanups, resets, or environment changes so you have an easy recovery point.

variableSyntax.title: What is the variable syntax?
variableSyntax.answer1: Prompt IDE uses double curly braces such as {{variableName}} to mark variables inside prompt content. The editor detects those placeholders automatically.
variableSyntax.answer2: For best results, keep variable names simple and use letters, numbers, and underscores only.

modulesVsPrompts.title: What is the difference between modules and prompts?
modulesVsPrompts.answer1: A prompt is a full instruction asset that can stand on its own. A module is a reusable fragment such as a role or formatting rule that is meant to be inserted into multiple prompts.
modulesVsPrompts.answer2: Use prompts for complete workflows and modules for repeatable building blocks.

restoreVersions.title: Will restoring a version delete my newer work?
restoreVersions.answer1: No. Restoring a version does not erase the newer snapshots already in history. Instead, the restore becomes a new latest version based on the older content.
restoreVersions.answer2: That means you can still inspect or compare the versions created after the one you restored from.
